title: Evidence Collection and System Isolation
description: Use PowerShell to collect process, network connection, and Security event log evidence from PIPELINE-DC01, then isolate the system from the network after evidence collection is complete.
context: You are logged into PIPELINE-DC01 as a domain administrator for ExampleCorp. The EDR has alerted to suspicious file activity on this workstation. You need to collect evidence (process state, network connections, logon events) before isolating the system. C:\Evidence\ already exists.

steps.instruction: Capture the top 10 CPU-consuming processes as the first evidence snapshot. Show Name, Id, CPU, and Path for each.
steps.hint: Use Get-Process piped to Sort-Object CPU with -Descending, then Select-Object -First 10 with the four fields.

hintPatterns.hint: Close — use Get-Process without a name filter to get all processes, then pipe to Sort-Object CPU -Descending and Select-Object -First 10 Name, Id, CPU, Path.
tasklist shows processes but is not a PowerShell cmdlet. Use Get-Process | Sort-Object CPU -Descending | Select-Object -First 10 Name, Id, CPU, Path for structured PS 5.1 output.

steps.successOutput:
Name                     Id       CPU Path
----                     --       --- ----
crypto_worker.exe      4512    94.23 C:\Users\Public\AppData\Temp\crypto_worker.exe
CPUMiner.exe           3908    87.11 C:\ProgramData\svchost_upd\CPUMiner.exe
svchost                1024    12.50 C:\Windows\System32\svchost.exe
CSRSS                   888     8.90 C:\Windows\System32\csrss.exe
explorer               2340     3.20 C:\Windows\explorer.exe
dwm                    1892     2.10 C:\Windows\System32\dwm.exe
SearchIndexer          2648     1.90 C:\Windows\System32\SearchIndexer.exe
spoolsv                1544     0.80 C:\Windows\System32\spoolsv.exe
RdpCoreTS              3120     0.50 C:\Windows\System32\RdpCoreTS.dll
taskhostw              2144     0.30 C:\Windows\System32\taskhostw.exe
steps.feedbackOnWrong: Run: Get-Process | Sort-Object CPU -Descending | Select-Object -First 10 Name, Id, CPU, Path

steps.instruction: Capture all established TCP connections to remote addresses outside the 10.x.x.x range. Show LocalPort, RemoteAddress, RemotePort, and OwningProcess.
steps.hint: Use Get-NetTCPConnection -State Established piped to Where-Object filtering RemoteAddress not starting with '10.', then Select-Object the four fields.

hintPatterns.hint: netstat -ano shows similar data but is not a PowerShell cmdlet. Use Get-NetTCPConnection -State Established | Where-Object {$_.RemoteAddress -notlike '10.*'} for PS 5.1 structured output.
Close — use -State Established (not Listen) to see active connections, and add Where-Object {$_.RemoteAddress -notlike '10.*'} to filter for external IPs only.

steps.feedbackOnWrong: Run: Get-NetTCPConnection -State Established | Where-Object {$_.RemoteAddress -notlike '10.*'} | Select-Object LocalPort, RemoteAddress, RemotePort, OwningProcess

steps.instruction: Export the last 50 Security event log logon events (Event ID 4624) to a CSV file at C:\Evidence\logons.csv. Use -NoTypeInformation to omit the PS type header.
steps.hint: Use Get-WinEvent with -LogName Security and -MaxEvents 50, pipe to Where-Object filtering Id -eq 4624, pipe to Select-Object TimeCreated, Message, then Export-Csv with the path and -NoTypeInformation flag.

hintPatterns.hint: Get-EventLog is deprecated in newer PS versions. Use Get-WinEvent -LogName Security -MaxEvents 50 | Where-Object {$_.Id -eq 4624} | Select-Object TimeCreated, Message | Export-Csv 'C:\Evidence\logons.csv' -NoTypeInformation
Close — add -NoTypeInformation flag to Export-Csv to omit the PS type header row from the CSV file, making it readable by non-PowerShell investigators.

steps.successOutput:
"TimeCreated","Message"
"05/16/2026 03:41:22","An account was successfully logged on..."
"05/16/2026 03:39:08","An account was successfully logged on..."
"05/16/2026 02:15:44","An account was successfully logged on..."

File written to C:\Evidence\logons.csv (3 records exported)
steps.feedbackOnWrong: Run: Get-WinEvent -LogName Security -MaxEvents 50 | Where-Object {$_.Id -eq 4624} | Select-Object TimeCreated, Message | Export-Csv 'C:\Evidence\logons.csv' -NoTypeInformation

steps.instruction: Evidence collection is complete. Isolate this system by disabling the Ethernet network adapter. This severs the connection to prevent further spread. Confirm: this is run AFTER evidence collection.
steps.hint: Use Disable-NetAdapter with -Name 'Ethernet' and -Confirm:$false to skip the confirmation prompt.

hintPatterns.hint: Add -Confirm:$false to suppress the interactive confirmation prompt: Disable-NetAdapter -Name 'Ethernet' -Confirm:$false
netsh and ipconfig can disable network interfaces but are not PowerShell cmdlets. Use Disable-NetAdapter -Name 'Ethernet' -Confirm:$false for the PS 5.1 approach.

steps.successOutput:
WARNING: Disabling network adapter 'Ethernet' will disconnect this session if connected remotely.

Name     InterfaceDescription                    ifIndex Status
----     --------------------                    ------- ------
Ethernet Intel(R) 82574L Gigabit Network Connection   4 Disabled
steps.feedbackOnWrong: Run: Disable-NetAdapter -Name 'Ethernet' -Confirm:$false — note: this disconnects the network adapter. In the simulator, it is safe to run; on a live system, ensure evidence collection is complete first.
